items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.

items.q: Is the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies a true Doppler shift?
items.correct: No — it arises from the expansion of space stretching photon wavelengths during transit, not from the galaxy's motion through space.
items.misconception: Yes — it is the same Doppler shift as a moving siren on Earth.
items.random_wrong: No — it is caused by dust in space absorbing blue light.
items.why: Students learn the Doppler analogy early and don't update to the more accurate metric-expansion picture in cosmology.

items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.

items.q: Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor depends on:
items.why: Correct answer: Area, separation, permittivity. “Charge stored only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Permittivity of free space epsilon0 mantissa (x10^-12 F/m, to 2 decimals)?
items.why: Exact answer: 8.85 x10^-12 F/m. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
